era_context: The early Roman Imperial period under Tiberius (14–37 CE), second emperor of Rome. The empire spans from Britain to Mesopotamia; Augustus's territorial consolidation is complete. In China, the Western Han dynasty has recently fallen (9 CE); Wang Mang's Xin dynasty rules. In India, the Satavahana and Indo-Greek kingdoms coexist. This is an era of relative peace in the Mediterranean, but frontier tensions persist on the Rhine and Danube.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse written records; archaeological inference required)
Interior of the Americas (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Germanicus's campaigns on the Rhine and recovery of Roman standards
events.description: Germanicus, adopted son and heir of Tiberius, conducts major military operations against Germanic tribes, particularly the Cherusci under Arminius. He recovers two of the three legionary eagles lost in the Teutoburg Forest disaster (9 CE), restoring Roman prestige. The campaigns are tactically successful but strategically inconclusive; the Rhine remains the de facto frontier. Germanicus's popularity with the legions and public begins to concern Tiberius.
events.certainty_note: Multiple near-contemporary sources (written 80–200 CE) attest these campaigns. Archaeological evidence from the Rhine frontier supports the military activity. Dating to 15–16 CE is secure.

events.title: Tiberius consolidates power; Sejanus rises in influence
events.description: Tiberius, now firmly established as emperor after Augustus's death (14 CE), begins to withdraw from public life and delegate authority. Lucius Aelius Sejanus, prefect of the Praetorian Guard, gains increasing influence over the aging emperor. This marks the beginning of a shift toward more autocratic, secretive governance and the rise of the secret police apparatus. Sejanus will eventually become the de facto ruler until his fall in 31 CE.
events.certainty_note: Multiple sources document Sejanus's gradual rise to power during the 15–20s CE. The process is well attested, though the precise chronology of individual steps is sometimes unclear.

events.title: Wang Mang's Xin dynasty faces economic and military crises
events.description: Wang Mang's Xin dynasty (9–23 CE), established after the fall of the Western Han, is beset by severe problems. Massive flooding of the Yellow River displaces hundreds of thousands and triggers famine. Wang Mang's radical land reforms and currency manipulations have destabilized the economy. Bandit armies and peasant rebellions proliferate, particularly the Red Eyebrows (赤眉) and Green Woodsmen (綠林) movements. The dynasty is in terminal decline and will collapse within a decade.
key_figures: Liu Xiu (future founder of Eastern Han)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) and Hanshu (History of the Han), compiled by Sima Qian and Ban Gu
Hou Hanshu (History of the Later Han), compiled by Fan Ye

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records are detailed and consistent on Wang Mang's reign and its crises. The Yellow River flooding and rebellions are well documented.
cross_references: Leads to 23-001 (Fall of Xin dynasty and establishment of Eastern Han)

events.title: Expansion of Indo-Greek and Indo-Parthian kingdoms in northwest India
events.description: In the aftermath of the Satavahana expansion southward, Indo-Greek and Indo-Parthian kingdoms maintain control of the northwest. Trade along the Silk Road is expanding, bringing Greek, Persian, and Indian cultural influences into contact. Gandharan art, a synthesis of Greek and Indian styles, is flourishing. The political situation is fragmented but economically dynamic.

sources.name: Numismatic evidence (coins of Indo-Greek and Indo-Parthian rulers)
Archaeological evidence from Gandhara sites (Taxila, Bamiyan)
Periplus of the Erythraean Sea (1st century CE)

events.certainty_note: Numismatic and archaeological evidence is abundant but does not provide precise year-by-year chronology. The broader context of Indo-Greek and Indo-Parthian rule is secure; specific events in 15 CE are inferred from the general trajectory.

events.title: Expansion of Roman trade networks to India and beyond
events.description: Following the discovery of the monsoon winds (c. 45 CE, but preparations underway), Roman merchants are beginning to establish direct maritime trade routes to India. The Periplus of the Erythraean Sea, written in the mid-1st century, documents this trade. Egyptian ports like Berenice and Myos Hormos are becoming major hubs. This marks the beginning of sustained direct contact between the Roman Empire and Indian Ocean trade networks, bypassing Parthian intermediaries.

sources.name: Periplus of the Erythraean Sea (mid-1st century CE)
Archaeological evidence from Red Sea ports (Berenice, Myos Hormos)

events.certainty_note: The Periplus is dated to the mid-1st century (40–70 CE), so it postdates 15 CE. However, archaeological evidence from Red Sea ports shows that trade was expanding during the early Imperial period. The precise timing of the beginning of direct Roman-Indian trade is debated; 15 CE is approximate.
cross_references: Leads to 45-001 (Discovery of monsoon winds and expansion of direct trade)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative emphasizes Germanicus's campaigns as a major military success and restoration of Roman prestige. However, modern scholarship (particularly Tacitus's own account, read critically) notes that the campaigns were tactically successful but strategically inconclusive: the Rhine remained the frontier, and no permanent territorial gains were made. Some scholars argue that Tacitus's portrayal of Germanicus is hagiographic, influenced by his later opposition to Tiberius. The economic impact of Wang Mang's reforms is often portrayed as uniformly catastrophic, but recent scholarship suggests that some reforms (e.g., land redistribution) had support among certain segments of the population; the collapse was as much due to natural disaster (flooding) and military overextension as to policy failure.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 15 CE is rich for the Roman Empire and China, where detailed chronicles were maintained. Roman sources are dominated by Tacitus, Cassius Dio, and Suetonius, all writing 80–200 years after the events; they are influenced by court politics and the authors' own ideological positions. Chinese sources (Hanshu, Hou Hanshu) are similarly retrospective but benefit from a strong bureaucratic record-keeping tradition. For India and the broader Indian Ocean world, sources are sparse and primarily archaeological or numismatic; written records are fragmentary. Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and the Americas are essentially absent from the written record for this year.

graph_edges.note: Germanicus's campaigns are a direct response to the Roman defeat at Teutoburg Forest in 9 CE
Germanicus's military success and popularity lead to his appointment to the East and eventual death in 19 CE
Sejanus's rise to power in the 15–20s CE culminates in his dominance of the empire until his fall in 31 CE
The crises of Wang Mang's Xin dynasty in 15 CE directly lead to its collapse in 23 CE
Early Roman trade expansion in the Red Sea sets the stage for the discovery of the monsoon winds and direct Roman-Indian trade
